speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May 3, 1995. The survivor's name is Margaret Lambert. The interviewer is Juliet Halpern. We're in Jamaica, New York in the United States, and this interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am Juliet Halpern, J-U-L-I-E-T H-A-L-P-E-R-N, interviewing Margaret Lambert, May 3, 1995 in Jamaica, New York USA. Interview being conducted in English. Mrs. Lambert, why don't you begin the story of your life with when you were born, where.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born-- I was born on April 12, 1914 in a small town in the southern part of Germany-- population 6,000. And I went to school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Town, Laupheim, L-A-U-P-H-E-I-M. I went to school there for 12 years-- no, for nine years. And then I went to school the next town, which was Ulm, and that was-- that ended in 1933. And I had a wonderful--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your life, your family's life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a wonderful time. I had my mother and father, and I had an older brother, about two years older than I. And once we got over the sibling rivalry, we got to be very good friends, my brother and I. And my parents found out pretty soon that what Margaret wanted, [LAUGHS] Margaret got. And they let me be my own because I was not the usual child.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sense, not usual?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very strong will, and whatever-- I-- I mean, not unreasonably doing things that I shouldn't have do-- do, but I, I had my own way of dressing. I didn't like what my mother got for me. I, I wouldn't wear it. And I was strong willed. And I had-- I knew what I wanted to do very early on in life. I wanted to be a coach, physical education teacher. My parents thought I was a little freaky with all of this business about sports that I was so very into. I skated. I skied. I swam. I ran. I climbed. Not the usual Jewish little girl, let's, let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the town, the Jewish life in the town, the kind of home you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Laupheim had a very large Jewish population. I mean, 300 Jews in a town of 6,000 is pretty, pretty good percentage. And everybody got along very well. There was no ant-- anti-Semitism that I know of. And we lived in a nice house. It wasn't super huge or anything. It was just a nice, comfortable house. And it was a very nice life. I had very many friends, Jewish friends, but I think I had more non-Jewish friends than Jewish friends because through my sport activities, there were more non-Jewish people involved than Jewish people. I was the only girl in my class. I was the only Jew in my class, and it never made any difference. I mean, I never felt any anti-Semitism until a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall feeling an awareness of your Jewishness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I always knew I was Jewish, but-- our house, there was no, no religion practiced, really. I always say, my father said be a decent human being. That should be your religion, and I think I have practiced that, pretty much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a synagogue in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, a very old synagogue, and-- which, of course, was burned down during the Crystal Night. And there were many-- it was mostly orthodox community, and the women had to be upstairs, the men downstairs. And that always bothered me, even when I was a little girl. I said, I don't understand why this has to be like that. And-- the only two--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family would occasionally go to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Twice a year, on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. That's the only times we went to the temple. And I went under protest because I had to wear a hat, which I hated. But, you know, it was a nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other relatives in that town or nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes. I had quite a few cousins there, uncles, aunts, you know, cousins, and we all got along very well. But as I said, I was usually outside of that circle because I had too much to do as far as my sports was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father doing in those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had, together with another brother and two cousins, a factory that dealt with anything that had to do with hair. He-- they, they bought hair from Italy and China and brought it in and prepared it, cleaned it, dyed it. And part of it was sent back to China to make hairnets out of them. And they made the toupees, and they made false beards. And they made theater wigs. Some of them even found their way into the Metropolitan Opera here in New York. So it was a very good business. He had about 120 employees, which was pretty good size for a small town like this, and life was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have an occupation outside of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She-- it wasn't-- I don't think at that time women worked. I know my aunt did volunteer work, but my mother didn't. And she was just, just a-- you know, taking care of us and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned you had no awareness of anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you first aware of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started in 1933, really. But I had very good friends of whom I knew that they were members of the Nazi party since 1928, and she was one of my best friends. She didn't care that I was Jewish, and I didn't care that she was a Nazi. She was one of my sports friends, and we got along very well. But then, of course, in 1933, everything changed, like overnight. You could not go into a public place-- no restaurants, no swimming pools, no movies, nothing. You couldn't-- you, you were not allowed in any public place anymore. And people that you knew wouldn't talk to you anymore. Friends that you had, they would shun you, most of them. Not because they hated us all of a sudden, but they were all afraid because that was-- that was the way it was. You talk to a Jew, you'll be punished. And it was-- it was very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you make of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was very hard. I was 19-years-old and all of a sudden I was shut off from everything. I got a letter from my sports club in Ulm that I wasn't welcome there anymore because I was Jewish. And that was-- that was the thing that I loved to do most, as I said before, sports. And all of a sudden, that was finished. There was nothing I could do. We just were really vegetating, if you want to call it that. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already competing in sports at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes. I started when I was about 10 years old. And it really accelerated to, to, you know, a higher level, and I happened to be very good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far had you gone in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Germany there was a different [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] method that they have here. For three years, I went to a Jewish school. All the, the Jewish children had to go to Jewish school. The Protestant children went to a Protestant school, and the Catholic children went to a Catholic school. And after that, I went to, for higher education a gymnasium, what they called. And I stayed there for another six years, so that was nine years. And then I went-- what's equivalent to college-- to college here-- I went another three years in the next town, in Ulm, where I had to commute to.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, I would have been ready to go to the university, but that was in 1933, and I had been accepted there, at the university of physical education. And I wrote to them that I was Jewish. Was it advisable to come at this point? And they wrote to me, literally, don't come until this thing blows over, meaning Hitler. Will, you know-- he will not last. But it didn't work out that way. So I never got to go and that was it. That was another blow. Was this something that was talked about extensively at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I mean, there was one Jewish rest-- two Jewish restaurants-- one-- but the one mostly where everybody congregated, and that was all we talked about. What are we going to do? Where are we going to go? How are we going to be accepted any place else? What are we going to do with our lives? And that that was-- people started to study He-- Hebrew, you know, to go to Israel. And it was a constant thing. I mean, that was the only place where we could go, and there was nothing else talked about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there at age 19, and then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the spring of 1933 and in the fall, we decided that this was no life for me. And my parents and I decided that I should go to England and see if I could find a school there where I could get an education in physical education. So we went to London, couldn't find a school that was good for me, and I just enrolled in a school to learn English. I figured that certainly, you know, will come in handy some day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a track and field club in the school, and when I was shown around in the-- in the school, they took me down to the gym. They were practicing high jumping. I mean of all things, that was my specialty. And my eyes started to bug out, and the principal said to me, would you like to, to do this? And I said, I would love it. So they found an old gym suit for me, and I had to jump barefoot, nobody could fit my big feet, you know. [LAUGHS] And that was the first time I had done this in so long, and I-- it was just-- it was heavenly, [LAUGHS] you know. And I don't think I ever jumped that-- that well because I was just so relieved that I was into this again. So right away, I was invited to join the track club, and that sealed my fate there. My father left, and I had found a place where I could get room and board, and I went to school. And what was nice, that I was accepted in the school. I had quite a few friends there. However, not once, not once was I invited to somebody's home, and I still wonder why not? I mean, why was I discriminated against in England? I just-- I tried to get away from that in Germany. Why was I discriminated against? I mean, I couldn't figure out why it was never invited to anybody's house even though we were very friendly in school. We had lunch together and, you know, went shopping together, all this kind of stuff. That bothered me, did not endear me to the English. So what year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in the fall of 1933. And I competed for the school and won everything in sight, and then, somehow-- I don't know how that worked out anymore-- they-- they enrolled me or entered me in the-- the British Championships, which was on June 30, 1934, and I won. My father had come to watch me, I thought. And I won, and it was great, and I felt wonderful about it. And on the way back-- this was outside of the town, outside of London. And on the way back to the station to go back to London, we were in a taxi, and we saw a newspaper, newspapers being hawked in the street. And it had big headlines, something about the Nazis, and we made him stop. It was the day when, what they called the-- when Hitler killed off about 2,000 of his own friends and men because they were rebelling against him. And we thought well, maybe that's the end of Hitler. You know, we felt very good about this as long as there's all this revolution going on. And we were very wrong about that because he only showed that if you did not agree with him-- I'm talking of Hitler-- you weren't going to live a very long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, we got to the hotel, and my father got very-- in a very bad mood all of a sudden. And then when we got to the hotel, he, he told me that the Germans had approached him. That I had to come back to Germany to be on the German Olympic team. And I said, why should I go back? I don't want to go back. And my father said, look, I don't force you into anything, but we were threatened. The family-- still living in Germany, of course-- we were threatened that if you don't come back, you have to-- you know, the consequences. They can't guarantee what's going to happen. So, of course, I went back. I mean, what else could I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was such a peculiar situation. A year ago, I was called undesirable, inferior, you know, by the Nazis, and all of a sudden I was supposed to be on the German Olympic team. So when I got back, there was no place for me to train. I was not allowed in the stadium, of course, and it was a pretty, pretty rough time. And I wasn't allowed to compete with the Germans, but three or four times in those two years, I was asked to come to an Olympic training camp where I met all the, the other hopefuls, and I always did better than they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whenever there was a bad press in the United States in-- about discrimination in Germany, I would always be allowed to compete someplace. And if you can imagine what it feels like to compete in the Adolf Hitler Stadium in front of only non-Jewish people, who most of them wanted to, you know, get at me maybe. [LAUGHS] They didn't have very nice thoughts about me. What is this Jew doing there? And when I beat all the Germans, that was even worse. And I was always afraid. I mean, I was afraid day and night that something was going to happen to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason why I was put on the German Olympic team was that the Americans, the French, the English, they all wanted to boycott the 1936 Olympics in Berlin. They said there's discrimination going on, and we're not coming unless you promise-- the Germans, you know, have to promise that there won't be no discrimination, and that's how I landed on the German Olympic team. I was to token Jew, the only Jew, the only Jewish athlete. And I don't mean to brag about this, but I was the only Jewish athlete of, let's say, [? worthless ?] possibilities, and this is how I ended up on their Olympic team.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can only say that whenever I went to those training camps, the girls couldn't have been nicer. They were so, so-- I mean, I was accepted 100%. And many years later, I wrote to one of them. I said, did you know that I was Jewish? You were all so nice to me. They said, of course we knew. But you were an athlete like we were athletes, and politics didn't mean anything to us, even though they were all in the Nazi party. You had to be. So. Did your family have any concerns about your participating?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure they did, but we never really discussed it because they knew how I felt, that I was afraid all the time. And they were afraid, too, for me and for themselves because you never knew what was going to happen next. You know, I said maybe they'll-- they'll find me some night and break my legs. What do I know? I mean, this is what you had to think about in Germany because there was nothing the Nazis wouldn't do to, to get where they wanted to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I'm hearing your story, you went along. You felt you had no options.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no option, whatsoever. The Nazi motto, do it or else, was quite persuasive. Believe me. You just did as you were told. This is why all the-- our friends wouldn't talk to us in the street. We had people, friends, come in in the middle of the night through the back door, you know, throwing stones against the windows. And we would know that one of them would be there. And we would open the door and throw all the drapes and everything that nobody would find out that, that somebody was in the house who didn't belong there. And it, it, it was a terrible time. I mean, you can't imagine the-- what the-- what the Nazis could think up to do next to you. Jewish families weren't allowed to have non-Jewish maids, for instance. Or, if there was a maid, you had to be afraid that she would hear something, and would denounce you. My little brother who was-- he was what-- was about six at the time, when he started to go to school, the best friends next door to him, they would start to beat him up and spit at him. I mean, they were-- they were all brainwashed. The children would brainwash the ch-- the parents. The parents would brainwash the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this poor little kid who was six years old didn't know why. Why was this happening? These children were his playmates for six years, and all of the sudden they would beat him up and call him terrible names. And I would walk him to school as often as I could. And I couldn't dare to touch these children. I would-- you know, they would call me all kinds of wild names. They would spit at me. But I couldn't dare to touch them. So it wasn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I always say the Holocaust means to American people mostly what happened in the concentration camps, but very few people know how degraded we felt after having been accepted so fully. I mean, in that town where I lived, my uncle was on the city council, and everybody was-- the Jewish people were all respected and mixing. And there was just no, no separation whatsoever. And all of a sudden, you were-- you were like a, an outcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family particularly involved politically when you were a youngster?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, not at all. I know my father always voted. I know that much, and my mother. But there wasn't that much going on politically like here, you know, with this Republicans, Democrats. Or maybe I didn't know it because I, I wasn't interested in it, but I know my father wasn't in any political party, you know, as a party member. You didn't have to be. You voted whatever you wanted to vote for. You didn't have to belong to certain party, as far as I knew. And when I was in Ulm in the school, a lot of the-- my schoolmates were in the Hitler Youth, and still, we were the best of friends. It didn't make any difference whatsoever. So it's-- it was all very, very peculiar, I must say. I mean the people-- in Ulm I wasn't known. So everybody didn't care that I-- you know, I could go to the movies or whatever I wanted to do. I mean, that was in early 1933. But nobody cared whether I was Jewish or not. It didn't make any difference. That's why it hurt, I think, twice as much, that you were-- all of a sudden, you were thrown away like a-- like nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were expelled from an organization in Ulm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to describe what that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the s-- in the spring of 1933, I had belonged to this sports club for since I started to go to Ulm to school and had won many medals for them. And when they-- In 1933, I got a letter. You're no longer welcome here because you're Jewish, sincerely yours. [LAUGHS] That was it. And that was the end of my sports career in, in Germany. I mean, I was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was an Ulm organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ulm sports club--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sports club--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I had belonged to for three years. And there, too, no anti-Semitism. We were all terrific friends. We had-- I mostly had non-Jewish boyfriends, so it-- it-- it-- it was just unthinkable what happened afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you described because of the external political pressure as the 1936 Olympics approached that you feel Germany wanted you back on the team?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, oh yes, definitely. And as I said, every time there was some bad press in the United States in the papers, I would get a-- a-- a chance to, to compete someplace. There was one incident where, in the New York papers, they wrote about that I was not allowed to compete in the German championships.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German sports organization was questioned. Why wasn't she allowed? Oh, she couldn't compete because she doesn't belong to the German Track and Field Association. But why wasn't I allowed to, to-- you know, I wasn't allowed to be a member because I was Jewish. So they, they, they got around everything in a very sneaky way. And as I said, they got-- whatever they wanted to do, they did, whichever way they could get, get to it. So was it to the extent that outside nations were threatening not to come to the Olympics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Many, many nations. And there were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think in-- in-- in Madison Square Garden, they had about 20,000 people-- a-- a-- a-- a mass protest, not to send people, not to send the team to, to Berlin. And wherever-- there was a lot of propaganda going on. Do not send a team to Germany because that discrimination is definitely going on. And there was, in the archives-- Washington, you know, National Archives-- I have letters here where the ambassador, American ambassador, wrote letters to the State Department what was going on. And I don't think they paid any attention to it whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does that mean you were aware of it in-- in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were aware of all this going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. Oh no. I was only aware after I came here. That's when I found out. We have friends and relatives here, and they kept all of the newspaper clippings where my name was in. And that's how I-- I became aware of it. I knew that I would not be allowed to compete in the Olympics. Only in an extreme emergency would they let me compete. And it would've been a terrible-- for Hitler, you know, how, how could-- how would he feel to have a Jew compete in the Olympics, and he, he couldn't afford to-- to have me compete. Let's put it that way. I knew, sooner or later, they would throw me out, or they would find a way for me not to-- not to compete.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they found that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very sterile. They found that way. I just got a letter. I wasn't good enough, even though I had-- two weeks before, I had equaled the German record with the greatest of ease. And as soon as the Americans were on the boat going over to, to Germany, the next day, I got, the letter was addressed on the 16th of July that I wasn't good enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're suggesting the relationship of the American team sailing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Germany was free to remove you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sailed on the 15th of July, and my letter was written on the 16th of July.
